response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Dealing with fake content on social media honestly keeps me up at night sometimes. I spend about an hour each day scrolling through my feeds, and I cannot tell you how many times I have seen something that just did not sit right with me. Living in New York and working as a cleaner, I meet all kinds of people, and I hear all sorts of stories about what they have seen online. It worries me deeply because my two children are also on these platforms, and I want them to be safe and informed. When I come across something that seems too outrageous or too perfect, I have learned to pause before sharing it. My father had his PhD and always told me to question things, even though I only finished primary school myself. That lesson stuck with me. I try to check if the story appears on other websites I recognize, maybe news sources I have heard about on the radio or television. If something claims to be about Islam, my faith, I am especially careful because I have seen how easily our religion gets misrepresented. I ask myself whether this aligns with what I know from my community and my imam. Sometimes I feel anxious about making the wrong judgment because there is so much information out there, but I remind myself that taking a moment to think is better than spreading lies. I also talk to my kids about what they see online. We discuss how people can manipulate photos and videos now, which amazes me given how technology has changed since I was young. When I am tending my garden or listening to classical music to calm down, I often reflect on how different the world is now. I think the key is staying curious and open-minded, which comes naturally to me, but also being disciplined about not just believing everything immediately. It is hard work, much like keeping a home clean, you have to be thorough and consistent. We need to protect each other by being thoughtful about what we pass along, especially in our communities where trust matters so much.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 60-year-old self-employed cleaner with primary school education but a parent with PhD, suggesting exposure to critical thinking despite limited formal education. High openness means receptiveness to new ideas and questioning. High conscientiousness indicates careful, thorough approach. High neuroticism suggests worry and anxiety about getting things wrong. Islamic faith and parental role create concern about misrepresentation and protecting children. Limited daily social media use suggests measured engagement. The tone should be thoughtful, somewhat worried but practical, drawing on life experience and family values rather than technical knowledge.
response_parsed.tone: Thoughtful, concerned, practical, and reflective with touches of anxiety but grounded in common sense and life experience
